evals.name: project-status-email
evals.prompt:
BLUF this for me:

Hi team,

Just wanted to give everyone a quick update on the Orion platform migration project. Things have been moving along — the dev team has been working really hard and making good progress on a lot of fronts. We finished the database schema migration last week which was a big milestone, and the API layer refactoring is about 60% done. The frontend team has been doing a great job too, they've completed the new dashboard components and are now working on the data pipeline integration.

On the vendor side, we had some productive conversations with Dataflow Inc. last Tuesday. They seem receptive to our requirements but there are still some open questions around their SLA commitments that we need to resolve. Sarah is leading those discussions.

I do want to flag something that came up during our sprint review on Friday. We discovered that the legacy reporting module has significantly more dependencies than originally scoped — it's tightly coupled to about 14 other subsystems we didn't account for in the original plan. The engineering team has assessed this and unfortunately we're looking at an additional 3 weeks of work minimum, which pushes our go-live from 15 June to at least 6 July. I know this isn't ideal given the commitments we made to the business. We will need leadership sign-off on the revised timeline.

Also, the QA environment has been flaky this week — some intermittent failures that the infra team is investigating. Shouldn't affect the timeline but wanted to mention it.

Let me know if you have any questions!

Thanks,
Mike
evals.expected_output: A military BLUF memo that leads with the go-live delay and need for leadership sign-off, provides background on the Orion migration, lists key facts including the legacy module dependency issue, and recommends leadership approve the revised timeline. Clean memo format with MEMORANDUM FOR / FROM / DATE / SUBJECT headers.

evals.name: meeting-notes-memo
evals.prompt:
Turn these meeting notes into a proper memo I can send up the chain:

Call: Logistics Working Group — 28 May 2026
Attendees: Col. Harris (chair), Maj. Reeves, CPT Donaldson, CW2 Park, SGM Okafor

Col Harris opened at 0900. Discussed current fuel consumption rates for Exercise IRON THUNDER. CW2 Park said we're burning through class III at about 140% of planned rate due to extended vehicle idling during waiting phases. SGM Okafor flagged that at current rate we'll exhaust our pre-positioned stocks by D+8, which is two days before the exercise end state. Maj Reeves said the backup option is an emergency resupply from Camp Stanton but that adds $180K in transport costs and requires 72hr advance notice — we need to decide NOW if we want to do that. CPT Donaldson noted that reducing idle time by 30% through revised marshalling procedures could extend our runway to D+11 but that requires a FRAGO and retraining the drivers, probably takes 24hrs to implement. Col Harris said he wants a recommendation for the Brigade Commander by COB today. Everyone agreed the combined approach — request resupply AND issue the FRAGO — was the safest option. Meeting ended 0945.
evals.expected_output: A military BLUF memo addressed to the Brigade Commander recommending the combined resupply + FRAGO approach, with background on Exercise IRON THUNDER fuel situation, key facts about D+8 stock exhaustion and the two options, and a clear recommendation with timeline. Proper memo headers.

evals.name: policy-recommendation
evals.prompt:
Rewrite this in BLUF memo format for the CISO:

Executive Summary: Privileged Access Management Policy Modernisation

This document presents findings from a 90-day review of the organisation's current privileged access management (PAM) posture conducted by the Information Security team, with support from external consultants Meridian Security Partners. The review was initiated following the Q1 audit findings which identified several gaps in our access controls.

The review examined access patterns across 847 privileged accounts, conducted interviews with 23 system owners, and performed technical assessment of our existing CyberArk deployment.

Key findings include the following: First, 34% of privileged accounts have not been reviewed in over 12 months, creating material risk exposure. Second, 12 service accounts retain standing admin privileges despite guidance recommending just-in-time (JIT) access. Third, our current CyberArk implementation covers only 61% of in-scope systems — a gap that exists primarily due to legacy systems in the manufacturing division that were excluded from the original deployment scope.

Based on these findings, the Information Security team recommends a three-phase remediation programme. Phase 1 (0–30 days): Complete access reviews for all 847 accounts and revoke privileges where access is no longer required. Phase 2 (30–90 days): Implement JIT access for the 12 identified service accounts and extend CyberArk coverage to the remaining 39% of in-scope systems. Phase 3 (90–180 days): Deploy behavioural analytics to detect anomalous privileged access patterns.

The estimated cost of this programme is £340K over six months. Without remediation, the organisation remains exposed to the regulatory penalties identified in the Q1 audit, which could reach £2.1M under current GDPR and NIS2 frameworks.

This recommendation has been reviewed by the IT Risk Committee and endorsed unanimously.
evals.expected_output: A military BLUF memo for the CISO leading with the recommendation to approve the £340K PAM remediation programme, background on the 90-day review, key facts including the three critical findings (34% unreviewed accounts, 12 service accounts, 61% CyberArk coverage), phased recommendation with timeline, and a clear decision request. Proper memo headers.
